content.text: GenAI systems require continuous operational feedback from the teams closest to production failure modes. SRE engineers observe latency spikes, timeout cascades, and token budget exhaustion in real time. Support teams see complaint patterns, escalation clusters, and the exact language users use when the system misbehaves. Systematically capturing and routing these signals drives improvements that user-facing metrics alone cannot surface.

attrs.alt: Operator feedback loop diagram: SRE and Support signals flow through Triage into model, infra, and process action tracks, then close the loop back to operators

content.text: Why Operator Feedback Is Distinct from User Feedback

User feedback reflects perceived output quality. Operator feedback reflects system health, operational friction, and the gap between the designed and observed system behavior. These two feedback channels complement each other:
Because operator feedback is low volume but high fidelity, it should be treated as a qualitative signal that unblocks improvements no A/B test can detect.

Every SLO breach — whether for p99 latency, error rate, or token availability — should generate a structured feedback artifact, not just a PagerDuty alert. The artifact captures why the SLO was missed, not just that it was.

import datetime, json

def create_slo_feedback_artifact(
    slo_name: str,
    breach_start: datetime.datetime,
    breach_end: datetime.datetime,
    measured_value: float,
    target_value: float,
    contributing_factors: list[str],
    recommended_actions: list[str],
) -> dict:
    """
    Creates a structured SLO breach feedback artifact for triage.
    """
    return {
        "type": "slo_breach",
        "slo": slo_name,
        "breach_window_minutes": round(
            (breach_end - breach_start).total_seconds() / 60, 1
        ),
        "measured": measured_value,
        "target": target_value,
        "overshoot_pct": round((measured_value - target_value) / target_value * 100, 1),
        "contributing_factors": contributing_factors,
        "recommended_actions": recommended_actions,
        "submitted_by": "sre-oncall-automation",
        "created_at": breach_end.isoformat(),
    }

artifact = create_slo_feedback_artifact(
    slo_name="genai-api.p99-latency",
    breach_start=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 1, 14, 32),
    breach_end=datetime.datetime(2026, 6, 1, 14, 58),
    measured_value=7.3,      # seconds
    target_value=4.0,
    contributing_factors=[
        "upstream LLM provider cold-start on new pod",
        "RAG retrieval indexing spike (12k docs re-indexed at 14:30)",
        "missing circuit breaker on embedding service timeout",
    ],
    recommended_actions=[
        "Add circuit breaker with 2s timeout on embedding calls",
        "Stagger RAG re-indexing to off-peak hours",
        "Pre-warm LLM provider connection pool on pod start",
    ],
)

Each artifact is routed to the feedback triage queue automatically. The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'contributing_factors', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  and  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'recommended_actions', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  fields are the operator's expert diagnosis — they must not be auto-generated by the alerting system, because they require context only the on-call engineer has.
Postmortems are the richest source of SRE feedback. A structured extraction pass after each postmortem turns narrative text into actionable signals.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class PostmortemFeedbackItem:
    incident_id: str
    severity: Literal["P0", "P1", "P2"]
    affected_component: str           # "inference", "rag", "routing", "embedding"
    root_cause_category: str          # "model", "infra", "prompt", "data", "dependency"
    user_impact_minutes: int
    runbook_gap: bool                 # True if responder lacked a runbook step
    slo_contribution: bool            # True if this incident contributed to SLO miss
    action_items: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

# Example from a real postmortem extraction
item = PostmortemFeedbackItem(
    incident_id="INC-2026-0614",
    severity="P1",
    affected_component="rag",
    root_cause_category="data",
    user_impact_minutes=34,
    runbook_gap=True,               # No runbook step for stale vector index
    slo_contribution=True,
    action_items=[
        "Add vector index staleness check to health endpoint",
        "Write runbook step: detect and recover stale index",
        "Alert on index age > 6h with warning severity",
    ],
)

The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'runbook_gap', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  field drives a separate KB authoring task. The  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'root_cause_category', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  enables trend analysis: three  {'type': 'text', 'text': '"data"', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  incidents in a quarter signals a data quality program gap, not an infrastructure problem.

SRE teams observe token budget exhaustion, GPU memory pressure, and throttle events that rarely appear in user-facing metrics. These signals should feed into capacity planning and cost optimization reviews.

import httpx, json

def push_capacity_signal(
    signal_type: str,      # "token_budget_exhausted" | "gpu_oom" | "provider_throttle"
    component: str,
    context: dict,
    feedback_endpoint: str = "https://internal.ops/feedback/capacity",
) -> None:
    payload = {
        "signal_type": signal_type,
        "component": component,
        "context": context,
        "source": "sre-capacity-monitor",
    }
    httpx.post(feedback_endpoint, json=payload, timeout=5)

# Called from a Prometheus alertmanager webhook receiver
push_capacity_signal(
    signal_type="provider_throttle",
    component="openai-chat-completions",
    context={
        "requests_throttled_last_5m": 1247,
        "estimated_user_impact": "degraded response times for ~800 active sessions",
        "current_rate_limit": "60k RPM",
        "recommendation": "negotiate higher tier or add secondary provider fallback",
    },
)
Support tickets must be categorized with GenAI-specific taxonomy to enable trend analysis. Generic categories like "product bug" or "feature request" hide the root cause.
Weekly aggregation of ticket counts per category reveals which root cause categories are trending — triggering engineering attention before they reach SLO-affecting volumes.
When a support engineer escalates a ticket to L3 or engineering, the escalation must include a structured summary — not just a ticket link. This summary is the support team's feedback artifact.

# Escalation report template (filed in Jira as a sub-task on the engineering epic)
escalation:
  ticket_id: SUP-48291
  severity: P2
  customer_tier: enterprise   # drives SLA urgency
  summary: >
    Enterprise customer reports that financial report generation produces
    incorrect totals when tables span multiple pages in the input PDF.
    Reproducible with attached 43-page annual report PDF.
  observed_behavior: "Totals in extracted table rows are summed incorrectly (off by ~12%)"
  expected_behavior: "Correct numeric extraction from multi-page PDF tables"
  reproduction_steps:
    - Upload 43-page PDF (attached: annual_report_2025.pdf)
    - Prompt: "Extract the revenue by quarter table and sum annual totals"
    - Observe: Q3 total reads $4.12B instead of $4.67B
  root_cause_hypothesis: "Document chunking splits table rows across chunk boundaries; RAG reassembly loses rows 14-17"
  components_suspected:
    - pdf-parser
    - rag-chunker
  support_engineer: "jsmith@company.com"
  first_reported: "2026-06-28"
  customer_workaround: "Split PDF into per-chapter files before upload"
Customer satisfaction scores alone are not actionable. The themes behind low CSAT scores are. Support teams should run a weekly theme extraction pass on free-text CSAT comments.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def extract_csat_themes(comments: list[str], model: str = "claude-sonnet-5") -> dict:
    """
    Uses an LLM to cluster CSAT comments into actionable themes.
    Returns themes with representative quotes and suggested owner.
    """
    joined = "
---
".join(comments[:50])   # cap at 50 to stay within context
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                "You are an analyst for a GenAI product support team. "
                "Cluster the following CSAT comments into 3-5 themes. "
                "For each theme: name it, list 2 representative quotes, "
                "estimate the count, and suggest which team should own it "
                "(model, infra, product, or support). "
                "Output JSON.

" + joined
            ),
        }],
    )
    import json
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

weekly_comments = [
    "The AI keeps giving me outdated information about tax rates",
    "Responses are usually great but sometimes just stop mid-sentence",
    "I asked it three times and got three different answers to the same question",
    # ... up to 50 comments
]
themes = extract_csat_themes(weekly_comments)

A weekly 45-minute sync between SRE, support, and engineering synthesizes the week's operator feedback into a prioritized action list. The meeting follows a fixed agenda to prevent it from becoming an unstructured discussion.

## Weekly Operator Feedback Sync — Agenda (45 min)

**Attendees:** SRE on-call lead, Support L3 lead, Product Engineering lead, PM

### 1. SLO Breach Review (10 min)
- Walk through any breaches from the past week
- Confirm RCA categorization is correct
- Assign action items with owners and due dates

### 2. Incident Postmortem Highlights (10 min)
- P0/P1 postmortems from the week (SRE presents)
- Flag runbook gaps → assign KB authoring tasks
- Flag recurring themes (3rd occurrence of same root cause = systemic issue)

### 3. Support Ticket Trends (10 min)
- Top 3 ticket categories by volume (support presents)
- Any new categories that appeared this week
- Escalations that need engineering investigation

### 4. Action Item Review (10 min)
- Review open action items from last week
- Mark completed, re-assign stale ones
- Escalate any blocked items to leadership

### 5. Capacity Signals (5 min)
- Token budget trends, throttle events, GPU pressure
- Anything that needs capacity planning attention
The output of each sync is a short digest distributed to the engineering Slack channel and filed in the team wiki. Every action item gets an owner, a due date, and a ticket ID.
Operator feedback items are classified by severity to determine response SLA:
SLO miss or enterprise customer escalation

Operators must receive acknowledgment that their feedback was acted on. Without this, SRE and support teams lose confidence in the feedback channel and stop providing structured signals. Closing the loop is not optional.
When an SLO breach artifact drives a code change, the on-call engineer who filed it receives a Slack message linking the merged PR and the expected SLO impact
When a postmortem action item is completed, the postmortem document is updated with a 'resolved' section describing what was changed
When a support ticket category drives a model improvement, a release note is added to the support team's weekly digest
When a CSAT theme drives a prompt change, the support team receives a before/after example showing the improvement

import httpx

def notify_feedback_resolved(
    feedback_id: str,
    feedback_type: str,        # "slo_breach" | "postmortem" | "ticket" | "csat_theme"
    action_taken: str,
    pr_url: str | None,
    expected_impact: str,
    slack_user_id: str,
    slack_bot_token: str,
) -> None:
    """
    Sends a Slack DM to the operator who filed the feedback,
    acknowledging that it was acted on and what changed.
    """
    blocks = [
        {
            "type": "section",
            "text": {
                "type": "mrkdwn",
                "text": (
                    f":white_check_mark: *Feedback resolved:* `{feedback_id}`
"
                    f"*Type:* {feedback_type}
"
                    f"*Action taken:* {action_taken}
"
                    f"*Expected impact:* {expected_impact}"
                    + (f"
*PR:* {pr_url}" if pr_url else "")
                ),
            },
        }
    ]
    httpx.post(
        "https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {slack_bot_token}"},
        json={"channel": slack_user_id, "blocks": blocks},
    )

Runbook gaps are one of the most impactful categories of operator feedback. Every time an on-call engineer cannot resolve an incident because a runbook step is missing, that gap is a latent incident amplifier — the next engineer to face the same situation will lose the same time.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class RunbookGap:
    incident_id: str
    missing_runbook_title: str
    symptom_description: str
    resolution_steps_taken: list[str]   # what the engineer actually did
    detection_time_lost_minutes: int    # time spent without a runbook
    suggested_alert_condition: str      # what should trigger this runbook

def file_runbook_gap(gap: RunbookGap, jira_project: str = "KB") -> str:
    """
    Creates a Jira ticket for a KB team member to author the missing runbook.
    Returns the ticket ID.
    """
    title = f"[Runbook Gap] {gap.missing_runbook_title}"
    description = (
        f"**Incident:** {gap.incident_id}
"
        f"**Symptom:** {gap.symptom_description}
"
        f"**Time lost:** {gap.detection_time_lost_minutes} minutes

"
        f"**What the engineer did (to be documented):**
"
        + "
".join(f"- {s}" for s in gap.resolution_steps_taken)
        + f"

**Suggested alert condition:** {gap.suggested_alert_condition}"
    )
    # (Jira API call omitted for brevity)
    print(f"Would create Jira ticket [{jira_project}]: {title}")
    return f"{jira_project}-9999"

gap = RunbookGap(
    incident_id="INC-2026-0614",
    missing_runbook_title="Recover stale vector index in production RAG cluster",
    symptom_description="Search latency jumped 8× when index age exceeded 12 hours",
    resolution_steps_taken=[
        "Identified stale index via `rag-admin index status` command",
        "Triggered manual re-index with `rag-admin index rebuild --async`",
        "Monitored progress via Grafana dashboard (15 min to complete)",
        "Verified latency returned to baseline before closing incident",
    ],
    detection_time_lost_minutes=28,
    suggested_alert_condition="index_age_hours > 8 for 5 consecutive minutes",
)
file_runbook_gap(gap)

The on-call engineer who discovered the gap authors the  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'resolution_steps_taken', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  in the moment — this is the highest-fidelity documentation possible, written by the person who just solved the problem with real context in mind.
Metrics to Track Feedback Channel Health
The feedback channel itself must be monitored. If operators stop filing structured feedback, the signal degrades silently. Track these metrics on a weekly dashboard:
Runbook gap tickets filed (of known gaps)

A declining categorization rate signals that support engineers are too overloaded to file structured feedback. A high mean time to close-the-loop signals that engineering is not treating operator feedback as a first-class work queue.

Operator feedback that identifies specific code or configuration as a root cause should generate automated tickets that block the next deploy of the affected component until the action item is resolved. This prevents repeat incidents from the same unfixed root cause.

import httpx

def block_deploy_for_unresolved_feedback(
    component: str,
    unresolved_p0_p1_items: list[dict],
    deploy_webhook: str,
) -> None:
    """
    Signals the deploy gate to block a component deploy if there are
    unresolved P0/P1 operator feedback action items targeting it.
    """
    if not unresolved_p0_p1_items:
        return   # no block needed

    blocking_ids = [item["feedback_id"] for item in unresolved_p0_p1_items]
    payload = {
        "component": component,
        "blocked": True,
        "reason": "Unresolved P0/P1 operator feedback action items",
        "blocking_items": blocking_ids,
        "resolution_url": f"https://internal.ops/feedback?component={component}&severity=P0,P1",
    }
    httpx.post(deploy_webhook, json=payload)
    print(f"Deploy of {component} blocked by: {blocking_ids}")
This integration closes the gap between the feedback channel and the delivery pipeline — operator signals become hard gates, not optional inputs.
Operator feedback from SRE and support teams is the highest-fidelity signal for GenAI system improvement. The key practices are:
— SLO breaches, postmortems, and support escalations must produce structured records, not just alerts or ticket links
— generic support categories hide the root cause distribution; taxonomy drives engineering prioritization
— synthesis across SRE and support signals reveals systemic patterns that individual incidents obscure
— notify operators when their feedback drives a change; without acknowledgment, the channel degrades
Track runbook gaps as first-class work
— undocumented recovery procedures amplify every future incident of the same type
Gate deploys on unresolved P0/P1 items
— makes operator feedback structurally impossible to ignore in the delivery pipeline
